title: Position Size Calculator: Stock Risk Management Tool
description: Calculate optimal stock position size based on account value, risk tolerance, entry price, and stop-loss. Includes Kelly Criterion.

schemas.headline: Position Size Calculator: Stock Risk Management Tool
schemas.description: Calculate optimal stock position size based on account value, risk tolerance, entry price, and stop-loss. Includes Kelly Criterion.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate position size in stocks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Position size is calculated by dividing your total allowable risk amount (e.g., 1% of your account) by your trade risk per share (entry price minus stop-loss price). This tells you exactly how many shares to buy.

mainEntity.name: What is a good position size for a stock?

acceptedAnswer.text: A common rule of thumb is to risk no more than 1% to 2% of your total account equity on a single trade. This prevents a single loss from severely impacting your portfolio.

mainEntity.name: How does the Kelly Criterion calculate position size?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Kelly Criterion calculates the optimal fraction of your bankroll to wager based on your historical win probability and reward-to-risk ratio. It maximizes long-term compound growth but can be highly volatile, so many traders use a 'Half-Kelly' fraction.

mainEntity.name: Why is position sizing important in trading?

acceptedAnswer.text: Position sizing is critical because it dictates risk management. Proper sizing ensures that you survive losing streaks (drawdowns) without ruining your account, allowing your profitable strategies time to work.

mainEntity.name: How does ATR affect stop loss and position size?

acceptedAnswer.text: Average True Range (ATR) measures market volatility. Traders often set stop losses as a multiple of ATR (e.g., 2x ATR) to avoid being stopped out by normal noise. A wider ATR stop loss reduces position size to keep total dollar risk constant.
